children.title: Default Title
children.metadata: Radiology article; Emergency department presentation; Metronidazole treatment; Abdominal distention; Fever and diarrhea; Laboratory abnormalities; Liver enzyme elevation; CT imaging; Breast cancer history; Peptic ulcer surgery
children.content: Amir H. Davarpanah, MD • Linton W. Eberhardt, MD<|txt_split|>From the Department of Radiology and Imaging Sciences, Emory University School of Medicine, Emory University Hospital, 1364 Clifton Rd NE, Atlanta, GA 30322. Received February 25, 2019; revision requested April 5; revision received April 22; accepted April 24. Adhere ascorrepond to AHTD (e-mail: aadanspae@emory.edu).<|txt_split|>Conflicts of interest are listed at the end of this article.<|txt_split|>Radiology 2020,295:733-735 \(\clubsuit\) https://doi.org/10.1148/radiol.2020190435 \(\clubsuit\) Content code:GI \(\clubsuit\) RSNA, 2020<|txt_split|>Mistry A 63-year-old woman with a history of left mastectomy for breast cancer and partial gastrectomy with Roux-en-Y reconstruction for nonhealing peptic ulcer presented to the emergency department and reported a 1-month history of abdominal distention; she was on a high dose of metronidazole, but had no previous history of abdominal pain. She was on a high dose of metronidazole, but she had continued fever, diarrhea, abdominal bloating, and loss of appetite. She reported no contact with a sick person or recent travel. At admission, laboratory studies revealed leukocytosis, with a white blood cell count of \(15.1 \times 10^{9} / \mu \mathrm{L}\) \((15.1 \times 10^{9} / \mu \mathrm{L})\) (normal range, \(0.48 - 1.27 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\)) and a low serum creatinine level of \(1.5 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\) (normal range, \(0.48 - 1.27 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\)). The patient was on a high dose of metronidazole, but she had no previous history of abdominal pain, and a C-reactive protein level of \(203.8 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\) \((194.0 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL})\) \((194.0 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL})\) (normal range, \(\leq 10 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\) \((\geq 59.2 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL})\). Liver enzyme levels were elevated, with an alanine aminotransferase of 48 U/L \((0.80 \mu \mathrm{kU} / \mathrm{L})\) (normal range, \(0 - 29 \mathrm{U} / \mathrm{L}\) \((0 - 0.48 \mu \mathrm{kU} / \mathrm{L})\), an apatase aminotransferase of \(98 \mathrm{U} / \mathrm{L}\) \((1.6 \mu \mathrm{kU} / \mathrm{L})\) (normal range, \(0 - 39 \mathrm{U} / \mathrm{L}\) \((0.16 - 0.62 \mu \mathrm{kU} / \mathrm{L})\), an alkaline phosphatase level of \(1.5 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\) (normal range, \(0 - 3.5 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\)), and a total bilirubin level of \(1.5 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\) \((25.7 \mu \mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{dL}\), normal range, \(0 - 3.3 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\) \((1.5 - 1.71 \mu \mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{dL})\). Adenobiprolenic CT showed Fig. 1 (3).

subnode.title: Default Title_2
subnode.metadata: abdominal distention; fever; diarrhea; leukocytosis; elevated C-reactive protein; liver enzyme elevation; metronidazole use; post-mastectomy; post-gastrectomy; nonhealing ulcer; renal function abnormality
subnode.content: Mistry A 63-year-old woman with a history of left mastectomy for breast cancer and partial gastrectomy with Roux-en-Y reconstruction for nonhealing peptic ulcer presented to the emergency department and reported a 1-month history of abdominal distention; she was on a high dose of metronidazole, but had no previous history of abdominal pain. She was on a high dose of metronidazole, but she had continued fever, diarrhea, abdominal bloating, and loss of appetite. She reported no contact with a sick person or recent travel. At admission, laboratory studies revealed leukocytosis, with a white blood cell count of \(15.1 \times 10^{9} / \mu \mathrm{L}\) \((15.1 \times 10^{9} / \mu \mathrm{L})\) (normal range, \(0.48 - 1.27 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\)) and a low serum creatinine level of \(1.5 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\) (normal range, \(0.48 - 1.27 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\)). The patient was on a high dose of metronidazole, but she had no previous history of abdominal pain, and a C-reactive protein level of \(203.8 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\) \((194.0 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL})\) \((194.0 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL})\) (normal range, \(\leq 10 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\) \((\geq 59.2 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL})\). Liver enzyme levels were elevated, with an alanine aminotransferase of 48 U/L \((0.80 \mu \mathrm{kU} / \mathrm{L})\) (normal range, \(0 - 29 \mathrm{U} / \mathrm{L}\) \((0 - 0.48 \mu \mathrm{kU} / \mathrm{L})\), an apatase aminotransferase of \(98 \mathrm{U} / \mathrm{L}\) \((1.6 \mu \mathrm{kU} / \mathrm{L})\) (normal range, \(0 - 39 \mathrm{U} / \mathrm{L}\) \((0.16 - 0.62 \mu \mathrm{kU} / \mathrm{L})\), an alkaline phosphatase level of \(1.5 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\) (normal range, \(0 - 3.5 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\)), and a total bilirubin level of \(1.5 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\) \((25.7 \mu \mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{dL}\), normal range, \(0 - 3.3 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}\) \((1.5 - 1.71 \mu \mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{dL})\). Adenobiprolenic CT showed Fig. 1 (3).

children.title: 2020 Diagnosis Please Learning Objectives
children.metadata: Diagnosis recognition; Pathologic identification; Clinical reasoning
children.content: In submitting a diagnosis for this case, participants demonstrate the ability to<|txt_split|>- Recognize normal and abnormal findings as presented in the diagnostic images<|txt_split|>Identify pathologic conditions indicated in the diagnostic images<|txt_split|>- Use clinical reasoning skills to generate a list of differential diagnoses

children.title: Accreditation and Designation Statement
children.metadata: RSNA accreditation; CME activity; 120 hours credit; physician participation
children.content: The RSNA is accredited by the Accreditation Council for Continuing Medical Education (ACCEME) to provide continuing medical education for physicians. The RSNA designates this journal-based CME activity for a maximum of 120 hours per year. Physicians should claim only the credit commensurate with the extent of their participation in the activity.

children.title: Disclosure Statement
children.metadata: CME Accreditation; Disclosure Statements; Conflict of Interest; Education Program Success; Relevant Relationships
children.content: The ACCCME requires that the RSNA, as an accredited provider of CME, obtain signed disclosure statements from authors, editors, and reviewers for this case. For this education program to be successful, it must be communicated that they have no relevant relationships to disclosure.

children.title: Submit Diagnosis
children.metadata: diagnosis submission; active case list; conflict of interest; approval required; deadline August 10, 2020
children.content: Submit the most likely diagnosis to http://rtna.org/depletion (use only for submission of diagnosis). Select the case from the Active Case List for which you are submitting and select the case that has been submitted by the author per e-mail submission. Multiple diagnoses and multiple submissions will not be considered. Deadline: Midnight U.S. Central Time, August 10, 2020. Answer will appear in the October 2020 issue. Authors wishing to submit their case for review should apply for permission and obtain approval for the case and further information.<|txt_split|>Disclosures of Conflicts of Interests A.H.D. disclosed no relevant relationships.<|txt_split|>L.W.E. disclosed no relevant relationships.

children.metadata: CT scan; abdomen; liver; spleen; spine; cross-sectional view
children.content: The image is a cross-sectional computed tomography (CT) scan of the abdomen, showing the liver on the right side, the spleen on the left, and the vertebral body in the center. The liver appears to have multiple hypodense areas, and the spleen is visible with a distinct outline. The vertebral body is centrally located with its characteristic bony structure, and surrounding soft tissues and organs are also visible. The scan is taken at the level of the upper abdomen, and the image is labeled with "c" at the bottom center.

children.title: b.
children.metadata: CT scan; abdominal cross-section; liver; kidneys; spine; medical imaging
children.content: This is a cross-sectional CT scan of the abdomen, showing the liver with multiple hypodense lesions, the kidneys with their characteristic shape and internal structures, the spine with vertebral bodies and spinal canal, and surrounding abdominal fat. The image is labeled "b." at the bottom, suggesting it is part of a series.

children.title: C. Figure 1: (a-c) Axial contrast material-enhanced (130 mL of IbaXol 350) portal venous phase abdominopelvic CT images.
children.metadata: Axial CT; contrast-enhanced; 130 mL iodinated contrast; portal venous phase; abdomen; axial view
children.content: The image is an axial cross-sectional CT scan of the abdomen, acquired in the portal venous phase after intravenous administration of 130 mL of iodinated contrast. The scan reveals the abdominal organs including the liver, kidneys, and surrounding structures, with the vertebral column centered in the midline. The contrast enhancement highlights vascular structures and parenchymal tissues, providing detailed visualization of the abdominal anatomy. The image is presented as part of a series labeled "Figure 1: (a–d)."

children.metadata: Abdominal CT scan; liver, stomach, intestines, bones
children.content: The image is a coronal view of an abdominal CT scan, showing the liver on the right side with a visible vascular structure, the stomach and intestines in the central region, and the pelvic bones at the lower part of the image. The scan reveals the anatomical arrangement of abdominal organs and includes the vertebral column in the center. The contrast between different tissues is visible, highlighting the contours and densities of the organs.

children.title: b. Figure 2: (a, b) Coronal contrast-enhanced [130 mL of lichenal 350] portal venous phase abdominopelvic CT images.
children.metadata: Contrast-enhanced CT; portal venous phase; abdomen; radiology
children.content: The image displays a cross-sectional view of the abdomen from a contrast-enhanced CT scan in the portal venous phase. It shows the liver, gallbladder, kidneys, intestines, and surrounding vascular structures. The contrast agent highlights the blood vessels and organs with varying degrees of brightness, allowing for detailed visualization of anatomical structures and potential pathologies. The image is labeled as "Figure 2i" and includes sub-labels "(a), (b)" for reference.

children.title: Figure 3: Sigmoid contrast-enhanced [130 mL of hefoxel 350] portal venous phase abdominopelvic CT image.
children.metadata: CT scan; abdominal; contrast-enhanced; portal venous phase; sagittal view; liver; spine
children.content: The image is a sagittal view of a contrast-enhanced CT scan in the portal venous phase, showing the abdominal region. The spine is visible along the right side of the image, with the vertebral bodies clearly delineated. The liver is prominently displayed on the left side, with its contours and internal structures visible. The portal venous phase highlights the vascular structures, including the portal vein and surrounding vessels, which appear enhanced due to the intravenous contrast agent. The abdominal organs, including the intestines and other soft tissues, are also visible, with varying degrees of contrast enhancement. The image is labeled as “Figure 3” and includes a note indicating the use of 130 mL of iodine-based contrast material.
